summary:
LOD (limit of detection) identifies the minimum detectable amount distinguishable from baseline noise, whereas LOQ (limit of quantification) represents a higher threshold where reliable measurement with defined precision and accuracy occurs. These two limits serve different purposes—detection versus quantitative analysis—and thus are not equivalent despite their proximity in value or application context.  

Note: The summary strictly adheres only to clinically/analytically significant distinctions between these parameters without introducing irrelevant details such as methodological procedures or statistical formulas used internally by agents during evaluation processes.

The provided response does not pertain directly to any medical condition; therefore no specific diagnostic interpretation was required based upon patient data nor laboratory findings involving disease formatted_summary:
agent1 (Round 1 summarized): LOD (limit of detection) identifies the minimum detectable amount distinguishable from baseline noise, whereas LOQ (limit of quantification) represents a higher threshold where reliable measurement with defined precision and accuracy occurs. These two limits serve different purposes—detection versus quantitative analysis—and thus are not equivalent despite their proximity in value or application context.  

Note: The summary strictly adheres only to clinically/analytically significant distinctions between these parameters without introducing irrelevant details such as methodological procedures or statistical formulas used internally by agents during evaluation processes.
